Q1: I tried to keep a structured routine, dedicating about four to five hours each day to studying across my eleven online courses. I'd usually start in the mornings when my mind felt clearest, reading through materials and taking detailed notes. But honestly, with limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access things online versus when I needed to work offline. Some weeks the routine held together well, other weeks my anxiety would spike and I'd find myself just staring at the screen unable to focus.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and how overwhelmed I was feeling. I aimed for consistency, but when multiple assignments piled up, I'd abandon the regular schedule and just focus on whatever was most urgent. The anxiety made it hard to stick to plans sometimes—I'd wake up intending to follow my schedule but then spend hours worrying about whether I was doing enough. Deadlines became my real organizing principle more than any routine I tried to establish.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or upcoming deadlines that got me to log in. I also logged in frequently to participate in discussions since I knew engagement mattered. Sometimes I'd log in just to ease my anxiety—like checking if I'd missed something important. With my high anxiety levels, I found myself logging in thirty times throughout the course just to make sure I wasn't falling behind on something.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost compulsive for me after the first few weeks. I'd wake up and immediately check for updates before even having breakfast. It happened because I was so worried about missing deadlines or important announcements—my anxiety basically trained me to make it a habit. The routine felt necessary for my peace of mind, even if it sometimes made me more stressed when I saw how much work was waiting.
Q5: The reading materials and discussion forums were most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd spend time carefully going through the texts and then formulating my thoughts in discussion posts—that helped cement the concepts. The video lectures were helpful too, though I often wished I could just read transcripts instead. Having twelve discussion posts throughout the course really helped me process what I was learning through writing.
Q6: I tended to minimize my use of supplementary videos and some of the optional interactive elements. With limited resources and sometimes unreliable internet, I had to prioritize what was absolutely essential. Some resources just felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept from reading. I know my assignment completion rate wasn't great at around fifty-two percent, partly because I had to make tough choices about where to invest my limited time and energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to exam preparation or when it deepened my understanding of core concepts. I'm very grade-focused because I need to maintain my distinction level, so anything that clearly supported that goal got my attention. Discussion posts felt worthwhile because they forced me to articulate my understanding. If something seemed like busywork without clear learning value, I struggled to justify spending time on it given everything else on my plate.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required activities had clear consequences, so they automatically got prioritized even when I was exhausted or anxious. With optional ones, I'd constantly calculate whether the time investment was worth it given my limited resources and high stress levels. Sometimes I'd start an optional activity but abandon it when anxiety about required work crept in. The pressure of required work always won out.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and reading materials, then study them on my laptop without internet to save data and work in quieter environments. I kept extensive handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly—that's really how I prepared for exams and got my 97.8 score. The LMS was just the starting point; the real learning happened with my notes spread across my desk, often late at night.
Q10: Most of my exam preparation wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, creating summary sheets, and mentally rehearsing concepts. When I was anxious before exams, I'd pace around my room reciting key points to myself. I also studied with a few peers in person, quizzing each other—none of that collaborative learning would register anywhere. My high exam score came from work that was largely invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access a reading or lecture once, download or take thorough notes, and then never return to the original source in the LMS. It was more efficient for me to work from my own organized notes than to keep logging back in. Given my internet limitations, this just made practical sense. My watch time might show only forty-three minutes for videos, but I'd spend hours studying the notes I took from them.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really difficult, usually when my anxiety peaked around midterms or when multiple deadlines converged. I remember one week where I could barely bring myself to open the LMS because I felt so overwhelmed by everything piling up. My low resilience meant that setbacks hit me hard—if I got confused by a concept or received critical feedback, it could derail me for days. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but they didn't make them disappear.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just scroll mindlessly without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities outside of school sometimes meant I had to choose between attending to personal matters and engaging with coursework. My seventy-five percent attendance reflects times when life just got in the way. Confusion about certain topics would sometimes make me avoid those modules entirely, which probably hurt my assignment scores.
Q14: Yes, quite often actually. I'd have the LMS open while my mind was racing with anxious thoughts about whether I was doing enough or preparing correctly. Sometimes I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I hadn't absorbed anything because I was too stressed. There were sessions where I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it registering. Being physically present in the system didn't mean I was mentally engaged—my anxiety often created that disconnect.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my completion rate was only about fifty-two percent. When something felt overwhelming or when I wasn't confident about my understanding, avoidance became my coping mechanism. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt less anxious, but that calmer state rarely came. The CBT sessions helped me see this pattern, but knowing about it and changing it are different things. Important tasks often felt too heavy to start.
Q16: Deadlines essentially controlled my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity in the days before something was due, then periods of minimal engagement afterward. My response time averaging around twenty-one hours shows I wasn't always quick, but I'd eventually get things done when the pressure mounted. Deadlines created both motivation and intense stress—they were the stick that kept me moving forward even when my motivation was low. Without them, I might not have engaged at all some weeks.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me temporary relief and sometimes motivated me to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, could trigger my anxiety and make me avoid that area of the course for a while. I'd ruminate on negative comments, which wasn't productive. The feedback loop sometimes felt more like a stress loop. I tried to use the CBT techniques to process feedback more rationally, with mixed success.
Q18: The course structure provided a framework that helped contain my anxiety somewhat—knowing what was expected and when gave me something concrete to work toward. Clear modules and deadlines helped me break down the overwhelming whole into manageable pieces. However, when the structure felt too rigid or when multiple structured demands collided, it became suffocating rather than supportive. I appreciated having discussion requirements because they forced me to engage even when I wanted to withdraw.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement much easier given my resource constraints. The discussion forums were well-designed and gave me a way to engage that suited my reading-writing learning style. What made things harder was when too many components required simultaneous attention—my anxiety would spike trying to track everything. Also, some activities required stable internet access that I couldn't always guarantee, which created barriers to consistent engagement.
Q20: I think that pattern describes me somewhat—I logged in frequently and engaged with discussions, but my assignment completion was only around fifty-two percent. For me, it was anxiety-driven avoidance of the actual submission process combined with perfectionism. I'd engage with learning materials extensively but then freeze when it came to producing work that would be judged. Some students might also be genuinely trying to learn but struggling with the execution part, or dealing with external circumstances that prevent completion.
Q21: This resonates with my experience in terms of exam performance. My exam score was nearly ninety-eight percent despite my inconsistent LMS activity because so much of my effective studying happened offline. Students who learn independently, use external resources, or have strong foundational knowledge might not need to constantly access the LMS. The system captures clicks and logins, not actual learning. I think I'm proof that engagement with the platform and engagement with the material can be quite different things.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns of access, timing, and completion of trackable activities. They can reveal when someone is active and potentially identify students who've disappeared entirely. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just clicking through. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or the genuine understanding that develops away from the screen. My distinction grade came from learning that happened mostly outside what the logs could see.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, connecting concepts to what I already know, and pushing myself to understand deeply. For me, engagement often happens in quiet moments with my notes, not necessarily when I'm logged into anything. True engagement also means caring about the outcome and investing emotionally in the learning process—though with my anxiety, that emotional investment sometimes becomes overwhelming rather than productive.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my constant anxiety about the course, the hours I spent studying from handwritten notes, or the peer study sessions that helped me prepare for exams. It wouldn't capture the times I was logged in but mentally paralyzed by stress, or the offline reading that actually led to my understanding. It also wouldn't show how the CBT sessions affected my approach to coursework, or how my limited resources shaped every decision about how to engage. The numbers tell a story, but it's not the whole story of my experience.
